serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve packaged lemongrass ginger tea is sold in a 250 ml bottle or can, equivalent to approximately 250 g, providing about 65 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Lemongrass Tea with Ginger (Packaged) — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for packaged lemongrass tea with ginger (cr_id D05010031). All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for packaged lemongrass-ginger tea. GI estimated at 52 (low) by analogy with sugar-sweetened herbal tea beverages of similar sugar concentration (~6g/100g) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 250g serving = (52 × 6.32 × 2.5) / 100 = 8.2. This is a conservative analogous estimate; actual GI may vary by product formulation.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage (6.32g sugar/100g) exceeds the 5g/100g SSB threshold; despite very low sodium (2mg/100g), habitual SSB consumption independently raises blood pressure risk.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid this packaged sweetened herbal tea and instead brew unsweetened lemongrass-ginger tea at home, as the AHA (2018) identifies SSB consumption as an independent blood-pressure risk factor regardless of sodium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 6.32g sugar/100g and zero dietary fibre; liquid sugar raises postprandial blood glucose rapidly with no fibre buffering.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this product because it is a sugar-sweetened beverage delivering rapidly absorbed liquid sugar (6.32g/100g, 0g fibre), which causes acute postprandial glycaemic excursions; unsweetened fresh lemongrass-ginger infusion is the recommended alternative per ADA dietary guidelines.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular SSB consumption (≥1 serving/day) is associated with increased CVD risk via triglyceride elevation and insulin resistance pathways; fat and saturated fat are zero, which is favourable.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this sugar-sweetened packaged herbal tea, as habitual SSB intake is linked to elevated triglycerides and CVD risk (AHA 2018); the zero fat content is a neutral factor but does not offset the sugar burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (6.32g/100g) promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity; the diluted lemongrass and ginger extract concentration in a commercial RTD beverage is insufficient to deliver meaningful anti-inflammatory gingerol or terpenoid benefit.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this packaged sweetened version and instead use fresh ginger and lemongrass in cooking or brew unsweetened tea to obtain genuine anti-inflammatory benefit without the pro-inflammatory sugar load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lemongrass Tea with Ginger (Packaged) is a commercially produced, ready-to-drink (RTD) herbal tea beverage sold in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. It is brewed from lemongrass (Cymbopogon citratus) and ginger (Zingiber officinale), then sweetened with sugar and packaged in bottles or cans for retail sale. The flavour profile is mildly citrusy and gently spicy, with the characteristic aromatic brightness of lemongrass balanced by the warm, slightly pungent note of ginger. The texture is thin and watery, typical of a lightly sweetened herbal infusion. Per 100g, the product provides 26 kcal, 0.12g protein, 0g fat, and 6.32g carbohydrate (all as sugar), with 2mg sodium and negligible fibre. Clinically, this beverage is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) due to its 6.32g sugar/100g content and zero dietary fibre, making it unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. Despite the well-documented anti-inflammatory properties of lemongrass and ginger in concentrated or fresh form, the diluted extract levels in a commercial RTD product are insufficient to confer meaningful therapeutic benefit. Unsweetened home-brewed lemongrass-ginger tea is the preferred alternative for health-conscious consumers. The product is vegan, gluten-free, halal, and very low in sodium.
common_user_queries: Is lemongrass ginger tea good for diabetes?
Is lemongrass tea good for blood pressure?
Sugar content of packaged herbal tea
Is RTD lemongrass tea suitable for diabetics?
Is herbal tea a sugar-sweetened beverage?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D05010031) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The food is an exact match in the primary regional source for Singapore foods. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable sugar-sweetened herbal teas.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database — this is a water-based herbal tea beverage containing no fat by formulation. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero for the same reason. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — the commercial brewing and filtration process removes all plant fibre. calcium_mg and iron_mg are confirmed zero per HPB source. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for this packaged herbal tea product are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry as of 2026. GI value of 52 is an analogous estimate based on comparable sugar-sweetened herbal tea beverages (~6g sugar/100g) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific product was found in published literature.
